head_coach.name: Jim Harbaugh
head_coach.background: offensive (former QB, offensive-minded HC at Stanford, San Francisco, Michigan)
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Physical, run-first identity; culture-driven, disciplined football; adaptable on both sides of the ball with strong coordinator empowerment

offensive_coordinator.name: Mike McDaniel
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Mike Shanahan / Kyle Shanahan wide zone tree (Denver intern under Mike Shanahan 2005, then Houston, Washington, Cleveland, Atlanta, San Francisco alongside Kyle Shanahan)

defensive_coordinator.name: Chris O'Leary
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Jesse Minter disciple (Georgia State GA under Minter, Notre Dame DB coach, Chargers safeties coach 2024 under Minter, Western Michigan DC 2025); carries forward Minter's Michigan/Ravens-influenced multiple front system

offense.scheme_family: Shanahan wide zone (McDaniel variant — speed-based, heavy pre-snap motion, conflict-driven run-pass marriage)
offense.base_personnel: 11 (with meaningful 21 personnel usage; McDaniel's Dolphins were among league leaders in 21 personnel alongside the 49ers)
offense.run_scheme: Outside zone / wide zone primary, with counter, power, and gap concepts layered in; McDaniel expands traditional Shanahan wide zone with more creative gap-scheme supplements and jet motion
offense.pass_concepts: Play-action heavy off zone-run looks, bootleg and naked boot concepts, quick game catch-and-run, vertical shots off play-action; Harbaugh praised McDaniel's 'emphasis on the run game and play-action pass'
offense.rpo_usage: moderate — McDaniel uses RPO elements but the offense is more play-action driven than pure RPO
offense.te_usage: Move TE / flex weapon preferred; McDaniel historically used TEs in multiple alignments — inline, flexed to slot, attached — to create formation versatility. Chargers have Oronde Gadsden II as a young receiving TE. The TE room was ranked 22nd in PFF receiving grade in 2024, signaling a draft need that McDaniel will prioritize schematically.
offense.rb_usage: Dual-threat back heavily featured; McDaniel's system made De'Von Achane the league's top receiving back in Miami (78 receptions for 592 yards in 2024). Omarion Hampton projects as primary ball carrier with receiving upside. Speed and receiving ability are premium traits for RBs in this system.

defense.base_front: 3-4 / 4-2-5 hybrid (odd front base that shifts to 4-down nickel frequently)
defense.alignment: Odd front base with 3 DL and 4 LBs, but predominantly operates in nickel/dime sub-packages; OLBs (Tuipulotu, Mack, Oweh) function as stand-up edge rushers; DL features nose tackle and two DEs who absorb double teams
defense.edge_rush_source: OLB stand-up primarily; Tuli Tuipulotu (13 sacks in 2025), Odafe Oweh (7.5 sacks), Khalil Mack (5.5 sacks) are the primary pass rushers from stand-up OLB alignments
defense.coverage_base: Multiple / no dominant coverage; Cover 3 (31.1%), Cover 2 (25%), Cover 1 (13.1%), Cover 4 (11.7%) per FTN data from 2025; mixes one-high and two-high structures extensively
defense.blitz_tendency: moderate — relies heavily on simulated pressures (9.5% rate, 10th in NFL) and stunts (19.8% rate, 9th in NFL) rather than traditional all-out blitzes; disguised pressure is the hallmark, not volume blitzing
defense.lb_role: ILBs (Daiyan Henley, Troy Dye) are coverage/run-fit players who also participate in simulated pressure packages; OLBs are the primary edge rushers; LBs are asked to drop into coverage on simulated pressures while DBs creep in as rushers
defense.safety_usage: Derwin James is elite hybrid — plays near the box on ~85% of snaps, functions as nickel/overhang/LB/blitzer; earned All-Pro at slot cornerback in 2025; Elijah Molden serves as second hybrid safety. This scheme demands versatile safeties who can play in the box, slot, and blitz.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: McDaniel's Shanahan-variant offense is built on formation versatility and pre-snap deception. TEs are used as flex/move pieces who align inline, in the slot, and split wide to create mismatches. The Chargers' TE room was ranked 22nd in PFF receiving grade entering 2025 and is a clear need. McDaniel's offense historically features the TE as a key play-action target and red zone weapon — a receiving TE with route-running ability and YAC skills gets a significant value boost in this system. Oronde Gadsden II showed promise as a rookie but the room needs an alpha. A Kenyon Sadiq-type TE who can win from multiple alignments would be schematically ideal.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: The Chargers run a 3-4 base where OLBs are the primary edge rushers. Tuipulotu, Mack, and Oweh are all technically OLBs who function as pure edge rushers from stand-up alignments. Khalil Mack is aging (34 in 2026) and the team deployed four OLB/edge rushers regularly. Any LB prospect with elite pass rush traits — burst, bend, power moves — fits this defense perfectly as a stand-up OLB rusher. Additionally, the simulated pressure scheme sometimes uses ILBs as delayed rushers, giving pass-rush-capable ILBs extra value.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: McDaniel's offense in Miami made De'Von Achane the NFL's top receiving back — 78 receptions for 592 receiving yards and 6 receiving TDs in 2024, with a 25.4% target rate. His system features RBs on wheel routes, swing passes, screens, and as checkdown options out of zone-run looks. Speed and receiving ability are premium traits. Omarion Hampton showed receiving flashes as a rookie (4 games with 25+ receiving yards) and McDaniel will maximize that usage. Any RB prospect with elite hands, route-running, and YAC ability gets a significant value boost for this offense.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: The Chargers defense is built around Derwin James as a hybrid safety who plays near the box on ~85% of snaps, blitzes, covers the slot, and has earned All-Pro honors at slot cornerback. James had 2.0 sacks and 8 combined sacks/QB hits in 2025, leading all NFL DBs. O'Leary was James' position coach in 2024 and built the scheme around his versatility. A safety prospect who can tackle, blitz, play man coverage in the slot, and function as a box defender gets maximum value in this system. With James entering his age-30 season, the Chargers may look for a long-term hybrid replacement.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: The Chargers are a rare team where ALL FOUR positional value inflections rate 'high,' creating a uniquely favorable environment for versatile, multi-dimensional athletes regardless of position label. On offense, McDaniel's Shanahan-variant scheme transforms how TEs and RBs are valued: a receiving TE functions as a de facto WR from flexed alignments (massive boost for Kenyon Sadiq / Colston Loveland types), and a pass-catching RB is a scheme centerpiece, not a luxury. On defense, the 3-4 base means OLBs ARE the edge rushers — any LB with pass-rush traits (Arvell Reese, etc.) should be valued at EDGE prices for LAC. The ~85% box-rate usage of Derwin James means the Chargers prize hybrid safeties above all other DB archetypes; prospects with LB/S tweener profiles (Sonny Styles type) get a massive boost. Mack's age (34) makes edge rusher a priority need. The TE room is a glaring weakness that McDaniel will want to address. Speed is the connective tissue across all positions in this scheme — McDaniel's Dolphins invested in speed at every level, and that philosophy will carry over to LA with Justin Herbert.
sources: chargers.com official coaching hires and press conferences (Jan-Feb 2026)
